data.title: APLY As A Tactical Reduced Risk Play On Apple
data.description:
The hypothesis — use covered calls as a step back from riding Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) if you are bullish. I will partially cover whether I am bullish on Apple later on in the thesis. But the emphasis is on cutting down risks as much as you can, tactically, for now. The proposition is more about how to ride AAPL now, than whether to. The core of this thesis might look like a philosophical debate of growth versus defensive plays in equities. But it is not—there are tactical aspects of my proposition. Bear with me—particularly the bulls!

Expect Volatility

The first point I highlight is the bull run in NDX from 2023 to the end of 2024. Almost two years. The lame mean reversion mindset calls for caution, and I agree markets don't always work like that. But risk-off plays (actually less risk than risk-off if using covered calls) at this point do not look misplaced in the least.

The political developments in the US, tariff war uncertainties, and economic worries certainly support a cautious stance from here on. Expect volatility and potential time corrections (with wild swings on either side), if not a fall. Of course, a bull rally from here on can happen too, but putting things in perspective, the odds clearly suggest no harm in cutting risks. Particularly, if it leaves a good amount of growth still open to claim.

For years now, mega tech has been more volatile than even a small-cap portfolio like IWM (which of course is also calmer due to its diversified layout across 2000 names). Apple has been extremely volatile while delivering growth through product super cycles. On the other hand, small and mid-caps, supposed to be the flagbearer of volatility in theory, have been relatively calm for the past many years now. That goes to show Apple's volatility levels, more than even some speculative bets in small cap. Not a revelation, and probably the way Apple has and will continue to deliver growth!

Whether Apple grows further or not, it will be volatile given its exposure to tariffs and geopolitical risks, high sensitivity to the uncertain rates environment, and retail/institutional crowding. Overall, AI hype cycle risks for big tech aside.

Use the Volatility

Apple's historical volatility, even after turning blue chip over the years, is good for option writing. In many of my earlier analyses on income ETFs based on option writing, I have emphasized how volatility is a key component for covered call option writing gains. The benefits of high volatility are in the premiums collected on wins. This helps offset larger losses bound to happen in the long run. Helps -"Don't eat like a chicken when you may have to give back like an elephant"! The probability of overall gains (holding gains plus income) increases with higher volatility.

However, there is a sweet spot. Too much volatility can often be a nightmare to manage covered call positions. Multiple scenarios, like months of downturn gains, where you gain on the option strategy (but lose on the core holding), wiped out as the underlying stock rallies sharply, leading to opportunity loss on written calls and capping underlying capital growth, make for an overall underwhelming result in the long term. I have usually kept my volatility benchmarks for the sweet spot or zone between Google (as the lower cut-off for volatility) and MSTR (as the upper cut-off for volatility). Apple's volatility is certainly not too high to be a red flag for option writing. But there is not much to choose from between Apple and Google, with periods of one being more volatile than the other and no set pattern.

Using APLY for Tactically Cautious Outlook

After meandering around trying to establish my point, this is the section where the specifics show up. Apple's historical volatility is established. But it remains towards the lower end of the sweet zone for a market-neutral covered call strategy like the YieldMax AAPL Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:APLY) to work. The reason I suggest APLY is its passive, relatively rule-based approach is far easier on investors than self—created option setups. I am not a vocal supporter of option-based income strategies in the long run (though it has its use cases, such as passive income plays). My thesis is for tactical use for the current setup. APLY makes more sense now as Apple's volatility is more likely to surge in a charged-up political and geopolitical landscape going ahead (reducing concerns around its historical volatility being high but could be higher for an options writing setup). Also, tactical plays when you are expecting volatility to exert pressure, and then support growth, reduce the need for volatility as the only indicator for APLY's applicability.

APLY works very well in sideways to correcting markets (i.e. it's underlying AAPL). Here I will expand on my overall take on Apple going ahead. A thesis on Apple's outlook deserves a separate analysis of its own. Valuing a stock like Apple, or most of the mega tech today, has become more about intangibles like positioning (sometimes even keyword changes in earnings calls) and narratives than traditional fundamentals. I can vouch for the pressures Apple is facing going ahead. China sales decline, service margin pressures, reshoring pressures, and tariff headwinds are significant worries to be cautious about. But I cannot vouch for the price action simply on the basis of the measurable fundamentals. However, it does seem prudent to dial down the risk exposure given the headwinds and overall rallies in the past.

Comparing APLY's performance to Apple's Total returns below shows why APLY is not a strategic long-term play when you are bullish on the underlying. APLY can only make total return sense if you wear the risk hat and start thinking of reduced drawdowns or tactical use, expecting a period of consolidation or correction.

Zooming into a corrective phase for Apple recently, I see APLY down by ~14.5% vs. Apple's ~17.5% correction. This is a relatively short period of study. Expect the differences to magnify if the downturn is drawn out longer. However, equally note that APLY does not make the ride Apple agnostic. The setup ensures that the drawdowns are mitigated by only a limited fraction. Hence, my emphasis on the proposition is not being defensive on Apple, but a step-down in risk terms if you believe in Apple long term and want to smoothen your ride tactically. APLY is still a growth strategy but dialed down while the pressures last.

Without Fully Capping Upside

APLY does not cap upside fully, though the upside cap effect is likely to trump the drawdown reduction in theory. But a performance difference of ~52% total returns in Apple at a time when APLY returns only 31% (as shown below), against the 3 percentage point outperformance in the drawdown scenario discussed earlier, is largely due to the tenure of the analysis. The actual upside capping differences vs. drawdown gains are likely to be far closer when compared across equal time frames.

The upside-capping nature of APLY is a risk to the thesis. However, observe that the upside is not fully sacrificed in return for better volatility management (or drawdown capping), particularly given the current environment for Apple.

Volatility Stays Flat

The other risk to the thesis is the assumption of higher volatility going wrong. In such a scenario, there is not much lost in using APLY as a tactical tool. In the below period, the opportunity loss in riding APLY through an overall consolidating market is very low. In fact, the period shown below did see volatility in between. In a truly benign setting, APLY should theoretically outperform APLE (as premiums gained on written calls, while underlying does not move at all).

Why APLY and How to Ride It

To summarize, I suggest shifting to APLY to ride APPL for the near term, as the odds of pressure on AAPL prices are high. Higher volatility may also be expected. APLY will save you more regrets on drawdowns than upside opportunity losses (less likely). The suggestion is still a growth suggestion, but a step-down.

There are nuances about "why APLY" over and above its option selling tactical use. APLY will pay out aggressively (not only the option income but a part of capital as well). Notice how the investments left on the table for riding AAPL erode over time (a $10K investment's path minus payouts). The aggressive yield of APLY at ~35% can be used in multiple ways, though. One school of thought for those looking to book profits or rotate out of AAPL for now is that this aggressive yield is a good systematic withdrawal plan from AAPL, de-risking investors with freed-up capital to invest elsewhere. The other course of action is to reinvest the payout for total growth. The flexibility to decide over a longer term, depending on how AAPL moves, works more systematically than bulk reactions.

My overall rating for AAPL for now is a Hold because, as mentioned earlier, fundamental pressures are not sufficient to call price action. APLY is a tactical Buy as its downside performance is acceptable, and it is likely to see outperformance versus AAPL in the current environment.

data.title: RxSight: Valuation Blowout Creates Opportunity
data.description:
RxSight (RXST) is a relatively unknown growth story, that has had a significant growth in the medical device space over the past 3 years. It's light adjustable lenses [LAL] are a big improvement to cataract sufferers with post operation adjustments allowing for significantly better outcomes. However, the shares have seen a significant decline since May 2024, as the sector has been weak and fundamental issues have pressured shares. After another destructive earnings report that missed expectations, the shares are at an interesting entry point for long-term holders. Long term the innovative technology to treat cataracts, a problem with a huge total addressable market, should see significant uptake over time. Growth is expected to continue at a solid pace in 2025 at just under 20% revenue growth expected, putting it in rarified air among growth stocks. The improvement in patient outcomes is a strong point towards continued uptake, with customers continuing to choose RXST for the best experience possible after surgery. However, short-term macroeconomic issues are a worry and allow for potential long-term gains.

Fundamental Performance - Macro Concerns

RXST has a long-term profile that is quite intriguing as an equity with high margins, a large addressable market and strong ability to scale. Margins of the light adjustable lenses are quite high, allowing for very high long term operating margins and earnings. CFO Shelley Thunen called out that it could be 80% or higher in the long-term leading to a potential premium valuation. The company is in a pure growth phase right now, which involves a large amount of marketing spend as they build the market out. Some may not be interested in the area due to the size of the niche, but the company is likely to be taken out in the long term and become a piece of a larger organization. Management points out that the overall cataract surgery market is growing 3% per year, with a very small number of premium offerings as of now. Long term, LAL could make up the majority of that premium segment which could grow to over half the overall market. The United States alone has 5 million procedures of which 1 million are premium cataract procedures each year. Eye care specialists are getting on board as RXST is helping grow the premium lens market, helping to boost revenue for those practices. Private practices will continue to lean into the most profitable options like the LAL with plenty of runways with just 15% of the 10,000 cataract surgeons trained so far on RXST technology.

Looking at the trajectory of growth throughout the past year, the multiple returning to low single digits at 4x trailing sales is providing a good opportunity for investors. While overall revenue growth in Q1 of 2025 slowed to 28% combined with softer commentary from management, the potential for growth is still there. Lenses per LDD (light delivery device) actually decreased for the first time almost entirely from macroeconomic pressures. Since the company is converting so many people from ordinary options, this means macro pressure is significant for RXST's sales. Margins, on the other hand, are a long-term tailwind, with LAL margins over 80% bringing company gross margins higher each quarter for the foreseeable future. This means a high 70% gross margin in the long term, helping achieve a potential 20%+ operating margin at scale. Currently, at -25% operating margin, the company will continue to burn cash at a moderate pace as it grows, and the sales organization grows to improve market position.

The lack of acceleration in placing LDD could mean slower growth over time, as the company starts to lap harder comparisons in future years. I still feel the company is capable of improving the placement by over 10% y/y which should support 2026 growth of at least 20%. In Q1 they placed 73 LDD against 66 in Q1 of 2024, so a growth rate of 11%. However, investors may be cautious about a lack of increased guidance in this area, and the speed of placement perhaps signaling a conservative approach from management. RXST is improving its marketing material as well as scientific material for clinics in order to help facilitate growth there as well. The macroeconomic environment is proving to be a headwind for clinics as well, which may be part of the slowing adoption of LDD recently even without imminent recession. Most don't have recession as a base case lately, but it is something to be aware of which could push down both LDD and LAL uptake.

Valuation went from sky-high at the peak of the post 2022 growth rebound, to now very reasonable compared to medical technology peers with this level of potential. As you can see, it traded at 20x its trailing sales in early 2024, with now back at a comfortable and fairly intriguing 4x sales after the recent selloff. This may have been exacerbated by tax loss selling in late 2024 for those that bought earlier in the year or later in 2023. The shares seem quite washed out now with all but the long-term holders having given up for something more in vogue at the moment, like a GARP stock or something focused on Europe. This does provide an opportunity for longer-term investors who can accept the swoons of an equity like this with strong fundamentals and a good cash position. Operating margins will take several more years to hit break even, as the company continues to sell the LDD at cost to increase the base of Ophthalmologists who can implant.

Risks - Growth Of LDD Device Utilization Rate

One thing that is on the mind of investors is the uptake of each Light Delivery Device [LDD] that allows for lens implants. The rate of growth has slowed and management has been telling investors to focus on the total number of LAL adjusted. This is a yellow flag for some as it shows management is trying to reduce expectations on growth rate or reacceleration in this area. Also, being a money losing business with a cash burn it has some increased downside potential in a recessionary scenario. RXST does have significant cash on the balance sheet at the moment with $237.2 million, allowing for plenty of runways to get to profitability for management. After a weak Q1 with an $8.2 million loss, this cash should help those awaiting a significant rebound feel comfortable an equity offering is extremely unlikely in the medium term.

Short interest has also increased due to the volatile nature of the small-cap medical technology space. Short interest is now 13% which is on the high end, although many companies in the space are seeing elevated short interest with risks to earnings from tariffs and the economy. This could result in some dislocation from fundamentals at further earnings reports until more clarity is reached on the long-term trajectory. It does mean the potential of a short squeeze in the future with a strong quarter or buyout rumor - but not something to base a potential investment on.

Buy For Those With A Long-Time Horizon

If you are investing for a 2-5 year horizon and can accept the volatility that comes from a money losing business, RXST has significant upside potential from these levels. It has the potential to be taken out by a larger medical technology player or can grow to a good size on its own as it continues to improve its market presence. Valuation is now at the very bottom of the range for a high-growth company, giving good valuation support to gutsy shareholders. At just $15.35 the shares are very intriguing for the second half of 2025 to funds and retail investors alike. Patience and an improvement in investor sentiment will allow for a significant upside opportunity in a fast-growing space with favorable demographics.

data.title: Nextracker: Leading The Solar Boom With Advanced Technology Solutions
data.description:
Introduction

Nextracker Inc. (NASDAQ:NXT) is a global leader in the solar tracker and software solutions industry. Revenues from the U.S. is approximately 66% and the rest from the international market. The main product line of the company is the NX Horizon smart solar tracker system, while its software solutions leverage the embedded solar tracker sensors, communication and control capabilities.

NXT has a market cap of $8.57 billion and is currently trading at $57.11. Its software solutions use advanced algorithms and AI to optimize the performance and capabilities of its tracker products. The company’s products are primarily used in utility-scale projects, which means a more stable revenue stream. So, I rate the stock as BUY based on the following reasons: the company’s momentum and its technologically advanced solutions.

Market Prospects

Overview of industry

Solar tracker is a competitive industry with many other players striving to gain market share. Specifically, NXT is the leader of industry with market share of 23% in 2023, as mentioned in this article. According to the company, its main competitor is Array Technologies (ARRY). It also competes with FTC Solar (FTCI), Arctech Solar and Soitec (OTCPK:SLOIF).

Markets prospects for solar trackers are more than solid. The global market was valued at $7.88 billion in 2023 and is projected to grow to $25.24 billion by 2032, exhibiting a CAGR of 14.32% over the same period, as this analysis illustrates. Also, North America is projected to grow significantly, reaching an estimated value of $9.58 billion by 2032. NXT is well positioned to capitalize on these market prospects and continue to expand its leading market share and revenue.

Financial Analysis and Momentum

Financial Analysis

Nextracker has a very healthy and strong financial record. The table below illustrates that the company’s revenue has grown consistently from FY20 through FY24. Meanwhile, its debt has remained at very low levels. More specifically, revenue has recorded an average annual growth rate of 21% over the same period. Additionally, the company’s debt consists mainly of long-term lease liabilities. This debt structure provides NXT with the flexibility to navigate through periods of financial stress more easily and less costly, without losing its overall ability to remain profitable.

The company’s profitability is already proven, as the table below illustrates. NXT recorded an average annual profit growth rate of about 59% from FY20 through FY24.

This strong profitability is mainly driven by the company’s low operating expenses, which its 5-year average is at 5% of total revenue -a signal that NXT has an efficient business model. Therefore, both its operating margin and its profit margin are at high levels, and they also appear to have an upward trend, as the table below indicates.

Momentum

Here, I need to mention the positive Nextracker momentum. NXT operates in an industry where projects typically have a long-term horizon, making the company’s backlog a key performance indicator. According to this article, NXT has consistently increased its backlog. This is a signal that the company’s products and services have strong demand. Investors have every reason to expect this positive trend will continue, as the below chart indicates. Moreover, countries and regions around the world have already shifted their priorities toward cleaner energy and have implemented regulations to support this transition. Great examples are The European Green Deal and China’s Energy Law -both regions where NXT operates. As a result, the company’s momentum remains positive and is expected to boost its future revenue growth.

Valuation and Target Price

Valuation Metrics and Peers

Considering the nature of the industry, it is better to focus on specific key valuations metrics and do a comparison with its main competitor.

Starting with EV/EBITDA and P/E ratios, both indicate that NXT is slightly overvalued than its competitor, as shown in the table above. However, the FWD P/E indicates an undervalued company, reflecting analysts’ expectations of high revenue growth. These expectations are also reflected in the 5-year CAGR, which is expected to reach approximately 20%, compared to just 1.8% for ARRY. However, the PEG ratio again indicates a slightly overvalued company, but this looks reasonable considering the strong future prospects that NXT holds compared to its peer.

Target Price and Assumptions

NXT’s target price calculated using DCF and EV/EBITDA models.

DCF Model

Assumptions are based on macroeconomic factors. According to this analysis, PV Solar technology expected to account for 80% of the growth in global renewable capacity between 2024 and 2030. Furthermore, the same analysis highlights that the cost of electricity generated from solar panels has declined dramatically over recent decades -a key reason why NXT’s margins have shown an upward trend, as previously mentioned.

Additionally, nearly 140 countries have implemented climate and energy security policies, as this analysis indicates. Further enchantment of this momentum is that global energy demand is projected to grow between 11% - 18% by 2050, according to this research. Most of this growth is expected to come from emerging economies, where populations are growing. Moreover, new sectors, such as data centers, need massive energy power. As a result, the projected growth in power consumption is significant, as the table below illustrates.

Take into account, the market’s expected CAGR of 14.32%, as above mentioned, a projected CAGR of 14.8% through 2030 seems reasonable. Also, the projected CAGR is in line with the company’s historical performance, as calculated from FY19 to FY24- as shown in the table below. Therefore, the mentioned expected CAGR seems to be also conservative.

In order to achieve the most reliable growth rate assumptions for the key components of the DCF calculation, historical data from the five most recent years was used. The table below illustrates the average percentage of revenue for each component, as calculated over the same period.

Finally, adjustments for non-cash charges, such as depreciation - stock-based compensation expense and deferred income taxes - were also taken into consideration, using with the same approach as above to calculate the final DCF target price. Based on the above assumptions and methodology, the DCF valuation results are summarized in the table below:

EV/EBITDA Model

According to Seeking Alpha valuation metrics, NXT trailing EV/EBITDA ratio is 11.87, which is below the sector's median by 9.86% (sector median is 13.17). Additionally, NXT is a growth company, recent valuation ratios seem to be more representative; therefore, I based my analysis on the average EV/EBITDA multiple over the last quarters, as illustrated in the table below, and a ratio of 10.70% appears more reasonable for valuation calculations.

The above CAGR and ratio, based on the assumptions of tariffs, will continue to be in low-level range and no crucial geopolitical event take place in the next 5 years. Also, a WACC of 7.90% and effective tax rate of 22.10% used to calculate the implied enterprise value. Based on the above assumptions and methodology, the EV/EBITDA valuation results are summarized in the table below:

Based on the above models, NXT's target share price is summarized as follows:

However, given the company’s proven track record and the strong potential of its industry, it is more reasonable to have more confidence in the DCF results. For this reason, the average of the above price estimates has been selected and the final target share price is $93.33.

Risk Analysis

Risk analysis includes two main risks: Geopolitical Risk and Competition

Geopolitical Risk

According to the company, a significant portion of its steel is derived directly or indirectly from steel mills located in China. Tariffs between the U.S. and China could have a significant impact on NXT’s revenue and growth. The recent trade tensions between the most advanced economies - including the United States, Europe and China - could affect the company’s supply chain, increasing its input costs. High tariffs and supply chain disruptions may have as impact in lower revenues, and is an aspect that the company can’t handle it with easy or cost-effective way.

Competition

As already mentioned, NXT operates in a very competitive industry. Therefore, its products and software must be able to differentiate the company from its competitors and attract customers. For this reason, any misleading services or the loss of one of its major clients could also negatively affect NXT’s revenue and growth. The company relies on large clients - as already mentioned, NXT provides solutions to utility-scale projects. According to its annual report, a single customer accounts for 17% of total revenue, according to its annual report. Therefore, NXT has high dependence on a few key customers.

Conclusion

In conclusion, NXT is an undervalue stock with a leading position in the solar tracker industry and solid financials, with an attractive track record. NXT can use its flexible debt structure to continue delivering high-quality solutions and beat its peers. Moreover, NXT offers technologically advanced AI-based software solutions, which is a competitive advantage. Given all the above, its current price looks attractive considering the long-term revenue prospects and is a buy option in my opinion.

data.title: Frontline At A Turning Point: Strong Fleet, Weak Momentum
data.description:
Frontline Plc. (NYS:FRO) is among the largest oil tanker operators. Although the company remains among the sector leaders in 2025, with a modern fleet and efficient operations, market conditions raise the question: is this still a good time to invest? After an impressive profitability cycle, 2023-2024, it seems Q1 2025 Frontline faces challenges: tariffs, increasing supply in the smaller tanker segment, and geopolitical risks remain high.

Fleet

Frontline is distinguished by its fleet, the company operates 81 tankers: 41 VLCC, 22 Suezmax, and 18 AFRAMAX/LR2 ships. The average fleet age is only 6.8 years, as many as 99% of ships are ECO class, and 56% have a scrubber installed. This fleet structure enables the company to remain competitive in the global market, meet regulatory requirements, and ensure lower fuel consumption. Frontline is focused on the SPOT market, making its profitability highly dependent on the fluctuations of the freight rates.

Q1 Financial Results

The results were notably weaker after a record 2023-2024, the company returned to a more normalized level of profitability. Net income was $33.3 million compared to $180.8 million last year in the same period. Although it is a significant annual fall (~ 82%), the company remained profitable even at lower rates. This highlights a resilient business model, but also that the market cycle is no longer at its bottom.

TCE (time-charter equivalent) was: $37,200 per day for the VLCC segment, $31,200 for Suezmax, and $22,300 for Aframax/LR2. The latter barely covered the cost, which was estimated at $23,300 USD/day. This means that the segment of smaller tankers generates very limited profits or does not generate any at all. Meanwhile, the VLCC segment, which has the highest exposure, shows stability.

Although the Q1 was weaker, the early Q2 trends are encouraging: VLCC rates are already over $56,000/day, which may mean that the next quarter will be more profitable. However, it is a positive factor, I can say that it is widely priced into the current price of FRO. The price is already YTD ~30% has made the company more expensive, and market participants are actively valuing the potential of the Q2-Q3 results. This limits near-term upside potential.

The Market

Market fundamentals remain healthy in 2025, especially in the VLCC segment. New ships in this category will be delivered very slowly—just +0.4% per year. Meanwhile, the Aframax segment will experience as much as +9% supply growth, which could result in oversupply. The supply of Suezmax will also increase by about +4%. Such a supply structure suggests that the frontline VLCC segment will remain the most profitable, but other segments may lose competitiveness.

The global demand for oil in 2025 should grow by about 0.7%, mainly in Asia. China and India are actively buying oil from Russia and Iran, long routes, which promote the demand for ton-miles. Russian exports that used to travel to Europe on short routes are now traveling around Africa to Asia. This extends transport times and, at the same time, increases demand for tankers. All of this works as a structural support for freight rates.

For Portfolio Building

Frontline looks like an ordinary shipping company operating in the tanker market. However, as always, I did a deeper analysis of the behavior of this stock, which reveals very interesting dynamics associated with oil prices. Using econometric tools such as the Granger causality test, Garch modeling, and OLS regression, I discovered that FRO is extremely sensitive to Brent oil prices and can be used in portfolio formation.

Let's start with the most important: Brent oil price changes statistically significantly predict FRO share returns. Using the Ganger test found that the price of Brent is "causing" FRO return with a 1-5 day delay, which means that investors responding faster than the market can use this window to open or close the trade. For example, when the price of Brent rises, the FRO shares are not yet able to react immediately, with the impact reflected in the next few days.

This behavior is important for active investors who use cyclic signals in the portfolio and look for tactical solutions. Unlike ETFs or larger companies, FRO responds with higher volatility but a time lag, so it is a great combination for both short-term and medium-term trading.

The FRO return Garch model revealed an important structural feature—the volatility is not randomly distributed over time but clustered. This means that one major movement often follows another strong movement. For the investor, this is very important: if the Brent pulse pushes the FRO price quickly, the odds are that the effect will last for several days. This allows not only to enter at the right time but also to maintain a position with increased confidence longer.

Periods of calm are interrupted by persistent waves of high volatility, confirming the GARCH model findings. For investors, this behavior means that once a strong move begins (often triggered by macro shocks like oil price changes), there is a high likelihood of continued movement in the same volatility regime, which can be tactically used to manage entries and position sizing.

  • FRO shares respond to Brent's changes with a 1-5 day delay, allowing you to use the information to your advantage.
  • The regression model shows a 0.31% FRO movement to a 1% Brent change, confirming the strong interface.
  • GARCH analysis shows that FRO volatility clustering, after the impulse, can be expected.
  • FRO can be used as a weighted alternative to tactical positions or energy portfolios.

Valuation

Frontline appears fairly valued, but it seems moderately priced compared to competitors today. P/E ratios are 8.89 (FY1) and 7.11 (FY2), which are lower than the sector average, but this is tempered by declining profitability and tariff volatility risk. Compared to other transport companies such as PAGP, TGS, or GLN, Frontline has the lowest Price/Sales indicator of just 2.09, indicating that the company is still relatively rationally traded.

Other indicators, such as EV/EBITDA (8.35 TTM and 7.31 FWD), also show that Frontline is not overvalued, but these levels are no longer very attractive. Dividend yields persist but dropped notably in Q1 2025. The amount paid ($0.18) was three times lower than a year ago ($0.62). Price to Book (1.71) and Price to Cash Flow (5.65) also show an average valuation, but not necessarily an attractive price.

In conclusion, Frontline valuation today shows moderate valuation, but is no longer a clear "value ​​game". For investors who want to buy only when the company seems to be undervalued, this price is too high. Therefore, this thesis is mostly supported by valuation and makes me assign it a HOLD rating.

Summary

It is important to understand that Frontline appears to be at the peak of the current market cycle. The company remains financially solid and has an efficient fleet. However, the high dependence on the SPOT freight rates means that any geopolitical change or demand disorder can significantly affect income. The current valuation, although not overvalued, no longer provides clear security to investors who expect strong returns.

For the tactical allocation, the frontline remains in a reasonable position, especially if there is a clear strategy for withdrawal when the cycle begins to turn down. However, this company is not ideal for long-term income investors or a fundamental axis of the portfolio. For those who have no position yet, the potentially better balance of risk and return can occur during a correction when the freight rates begin to grow. For now, a hold-and-monitor approach is warranted. However, valuation, limited growth potential, and emerging risks justify a HOLD rating.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: AI Upside, Strong Yield, 1.7X Coverage
data.description:
Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE:EPD) offers dividend investors a high degree of cash flow and return resilience, which makes the midstream enterprise a highly recession-resistant business. Enterprise Products Partners also succeeds in consistently growing its cash flow over time while maintaining excess distribution coverage... which will give the MLP room to grow its distribution. With demand for energy only set to grow in the years ahead, in part due to the growing energy needs of the Data Center market, Enterprise Products Partners has attractive long-term DCF and distribution growth prospects.

Previous rating

I rated units of Enterprise Product Partners a strong buy-in March — A Rock Solid MLP Growth Play — as the midstream company is one of the largest master limited partnerships in North America with a dependable record of growing its distributions. In the first quarter, Enterprise Products Partners continued to excel in terms of distribution coverage and raised its dividend by 3.9% year-over-year. EPD's units sold off in April after the U.S. announced new import tariffs, and although units have somewhat recovered, they can be bought at a cheaper price than in March.

Highly resilient DCF trajectory, strong distribution coverage

Enterprise Products Partners is one of the largest MLPs in the U.S. and owns a collection of highly critical energy assets including natural gas, crude oil, refined products, and NGL pipelines. The midstream company also owned complementary energy assets such as deep-water docks, for energy raw materials, fractionation facilities, and storage tanks.

Natural gas liquids are the single biggest business for Enterprise Products Partners, and the midstream enterprise generated more than half of its $10.0B in gross operating margins — in the last trailing twelve months period — from NGL transportation and storage services.

In the first quarter, EPD's gross operating margin totaled $2.4B, showing a slight decline of 2.4% year-over-year, mainly due to lower average sales margins, lower deficiency revenues, and a decrease in ethylene export volumes in the petrochemical segment.

Enterprise Products Partners has been able to consistently expand its distributable cash flow, however, through organic growth in its core business, pipelines, and acquisitions. Last year, Enterprise Products Partners acquired pipeline operator Piñon Midstream for close to $1.0B to strengthen its market position in the Delaware Basin in New Mexico and Texas. In the first quarter, Enterprise Products Partners generated $2.0B in distributable cash flow, showing 5% year-over-year growth.

A key growth driver for Enterprise Products Partners going forward could be the aggressive build-out of Data Center infrastructure around the world in order to accommodate progress in artificial intelligence development. Business consulting firm McKinsey estimates that close to $50B will be spent just on building Data Centers, a big portion of which are going to be built in the U.S.

Enterprise Products Partners itself has listed Data Center growth as a catalyst for energy production. In fact, AI and Data Centers are named as the biggest drivers of incremental energy demand until the end of the decade, which creates a long-term catalyst for EPD's distributable cash flow growth. The increasing demand profile also suggests that Enterprise Products Partners is going to continue to seek acquisitions to broaden its pipeline footprint, especially into regions that serve the Data Center industry.

Going forward, I would expect EPD to specifically acquire pipeline capacity that strengthens its footprint in the Northeast of the U.S. which is where many Data Centers are located. Further, Enterprise Products Partners

Stable long-term investment returns and excess distribution coverage

EPD has been able to grow its DCF, while maintaining stable excess distribution coverage, in the last twenty years, however. Increasing macroeconomic uncertainty including the financial crisis, the crude oil price correction of 2016 as well as the COVID-19 pandemic all did not hurt the midstream firm's DCF growth prospects for long...

The main reason for the stability of EPD's investment returns is that the company enters its fee-based delivery contracts with its producer clients, which means the midstream enterprise has very low earnings and distributable cash flow volatility. In Q1'25, about 80% of the firm's gross operating margin was related to fee-based arrangements, showing 2 PP growth compared to FY 2024.

At the same time, Enterprise Products Partners has demonstrated an ability to maintain stable investment returns over the course of a business cycle, including during periods of arguably increasing macroeconomic stress, such as during the COVID-19 pandemic. Over the course of the last twenty years, Enterprise Products Partners generated an average return on invested capital of ~12% annually, with very low volatility in the return pattern. Enterprise Products Partners' historical investment returns have fluctuated in a very narrow range of 10-13% which creates the perfect foundation, together with a high percentage of fee-based contracts, to support a strategy of high capital returns.

What supports the investment case for Enterprise Products Partners is that natural gas demand is expected to grow at least until the end of the decade (and likely beyond that) which creates attractive long-term DCF and distribution growth prospects for unitholders of EPD. According to Kinder Morgan (KMI), a key pipeline operator in North America, natural gas demand is set to rise, driven by growing domestic industrial and power demand. Growing energy needs strongly suggest that EPD will build or acquire more pipeline capacity in the years ahead, especially in markets that are connecting high-potential plays like the Permian Basin.

In the long term, Enterprise Products Partners has grown key financial metrics like revenue and EBITDA very consistently... which is the result of a high representation of fee contracts in its business. I would expect EPD to continue to grow key measures like EBITDA at mid single-digits in the years ahead and expect a similar growth rate for the distribution.

Low earnings volatility and distribution growth

Low earnings and investment return volatility obviously translate to strong distribution coverage and DCF predictability... all of which work to lower capital risks for investors and ensure that unit holders are receiving a steady stream of income.

In terms of distribution coverage, Enterprise Product Partners offers exceptional value for dividend investors. In Q1'25, EPD achieved 1.7X distribution coverage, which compares to a distribution coverage ratio of 1.8X in Q4'24 and 1.7X for the entire fiscal year.

Due to its high distribution coverage and, in part, acquisition-driven DCF growth, Enterprise Products Partners raised its distribution a total of 3.9% year-over-year to a current quarterly rate of $0.535 per unit.

EPD’s valuation

Units of Enterprise Products Partners have sold off drastically during the April ‘tariff crash’, but have since staged a solid rebound and are trading almost back at the pre-crisis valuation. Currently, units of Enterprise Products Partners are valued at an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 9.7X, compared to a 3-year average EV-to-EBITDA ratio of 9.5X. Investors therefore pay a very small premium to the historical valuation of 2%.

Rival midstream companies like Kinder Morgan, Energy Transfer (ET), and Enbridge (ENB) are valued at enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratios between 8.0X at the low-end (for Energy Transfer) and up to 12.3X for Enbridge. I generally like the midstream sector for its distributable cash flow visibility as well as low variability in returns... which makes pipeline operators perfect investments for retirees who have a need for highly predictable income certainty.

Considering that Enterprise Products Partners is expected to grow its EBITDA ~6% next year to $10.8B and that the distribution is set to grow in the years ahead, I believe units are still an attractive buy at the current valuation. Enterprise Products Partners is also likely going to grow its pipeline network through new targeted acquisitions, potentially in the high-potential Permian Basin, and with a high degree of excess distribution coverage present, I believe units could revalue to an 11-12X EV-to-EBITDA ratio... implying a fair value range of $35-39 per unit.

Risks with EPD

Enterprise Products Partners is growing its EBITDA and distributable cash flow and has through its growth projects a high degree of earnings and DCF visibility that I feel lowers risks for the midstream platform and its investors. What would change my mind about Enterprise Products Partners is if the midstream were to see a deterioration in its distribution coverage ratio (to less than 1.5X DCF) or if its distribution were to grow at a slower rate going forward.

Final thoughts

In my opinion, Enterprise Products Partners remains a no-brainer buy for dividend investors who value a high degree of distributable cash flow visibility and excess dividend coverage that gives the company leeway to increase its distribution going forward. Investors can reasonably expect a mid-single-digit growth rate in its distributable cash flow as well as distribution rate in 2025 and beyond, while the high degree of excess coverage should provide a limit on the MLP's downside. With shares trading for less than 10X EBITDA, I believe EPD remains a rock-solid choice for dividend investors and investors potentially get some capital appreciation potential here as well.

data.title: TSLT: Robotaxi Is A Big Catalyst For Tesla, But Maintain Sell On TSLT
data.description:
I last covered the T-REX 2X Long Tesla Daily Target ETF (BATS:TSLT) in early 2024 with a sell rating, as I was concerned about the TSLT ETF's decay from volatility.

For most of the past year, my recommendation had looked like a disaster, as Tesla's shares soared after President Trump was re-elected to the White House and "First Buddy" Elon Musk's fortunes looked bright. At one point in late 2024, the TSLT ETF had soared by over 300% since my article (Figure 1).

However, in recent days, the TSLT ETF has plummeted back to reality, as the Musk-Trump relationship soured, with Elon Musk seemingly calling for the impeachment of the President in one of his tweets on June 5th, 2025 (Figure 2).

As one can imagine, Tesla (TSLA) shares did not respond positively to the deterioration in relations between Elon Musk and Donald Trump, with TSLA shares plummeting by 14% on June 5th and TSLT shares plunging by 28% in sympathy.

TSLT's volatility over the past year encapsulates perfectly the risks and opportunities with leveraged ETFs. In my opinion, retail investors should stay away from these products.

At the end of the day, Elon's outburst may alienate his right-wing supporters, since many have sworn loyalty to Donald Trump above all else. Since Elon has already alienated progressives with his involvement in the Trump administration, losing conservative consumers could be a huge blow to Tesla's fortunes.

I continue to rate TSLT a sell.

Brief Fund Overview

The T-REX 2X Long Tesla Daily Target ETF is a leveraged single-stock ETF that aims to provide daily returns that are twice the return of Tesla, Inc. stock. The TSLT ETF achieves this levered return by holding cash plus total return swaps struck with large investment banks that are reset nightly to maintain 200% exposure to net assets (Figure 3).

As TSLA shares soared, the TSLT ETF had gotten quite popular with short-term speculators. At one point, the TSLT ETF had nearly $1 billion in assets under management ("AUM"), as investors loved the financial leverage of the product (Figure 4).

Leverage Works Both Ways

However, leverage works both ways, and as Tesla's vehicle sales slipped in recent months due to backlash against Elon Musk's perceived involvement in the Trump administration, TSLA shares have fallen by over 26% YTD, leading to a painful 62% plunge in TSLT shares (Figure 5).

Readers may wonder, why is the TSLT ETF down 62% when the underlying stock is only down 26% YTD? Isn't that 2.4x downside capture?

TSLT's problem is due to the 'daily reset' feature of the fund. Imagine if we had started with $100 invested in the TSLT ETF. If TSLA shares returned 5% on day 1, our investment in the TSLT ETF (ignoring friction costs) will grow to $110 (2 times 5% return). However, if TSLA shares lost 5% on day 2, our investment in the TSLT ETF will fall to $99.00, less than twice the 2-day compounded loss of 0.25% or $99.50. This effect is commonly called 'volatility decay'.

While volatility decay may seem small on a day-to-day basis, when we compound the effect over many days, volatility decay can cause leveraged ETFs like the TSLT ETF to significantly underperform their expected returns.

In fact, according to the manager's estimates, if volatility is high enough, a positive return in TSLA shares may actually turn into negative returns for the TSLT ETF. For example, at a volatility rate of 75%, a 30% one-year return in TSLA shares may become a 3.7% loss in the TSLT ETF (Figure 6).

This volatility decay 'feature' of the TSLT ETF is the primary reason why I assigned a sell rating to the TSLT ETF in my initiation article.

TSLT May Be Useful As A Swing Vehicle

However, it is not all bad news. As can be seen in TSLT's epic 2024 rally, if one has the foresight to buy the TSLT ETF at the lows, one can benefit from another feature of leveraged ETFs, namely, 'convexity'.

Going back to our numerical example above, assuming on day 2, TSLA shares gained 5% instead of losing 5%. Our investment in the TSLT ETF will grow to $121.00, more than twice the theoretical 2-day compounded return of 10.25% or $120.50. When streaky stocks like TSLA go on a multi-week run, a leveraged ETF like TSLT can produce significantly higher returns than 2x the underlying stock.

However, in my opinion, the only real 'winners' in leveraged ETFs are the issuers and market makers, who get to collect fat management fees and trading commissions as speculators buy and sell these products in a frenzy.

For most investors, I believe the best strategy on leveraged ETFs is to stay away.

Investors considering the TSLT ETF should make sure they read and understand these warnings from FINRA and the SEC.

Tesla's Vehicle Fortunes May Be Dimming

As I mentioned above, Tesla's sales performance has been poor in 2025. I believe one of the main reasons has been the company's stale product portfolio, which has been static for several years while competitors have been releasing exciting new models (Figure 7).

The most recent major vehicle release by Tesla was the Cybertruck in 2023, which has been a huge disappointment, in my opinion. Recall, when Elon Musk first teased the Cybertruck in 2019, Tesla boasted about thousands of pre-orders from fans of the EV-maker.

In fact, at launch, Tesla had a reported 1.9 million vehicle orderbook and 5-year wait times for the Cybertruck. Elon Musk predicted that Tesla would sell 250,000 Cybertrucks every year. However, almost 2 years since launch, the Cybertruck has sold less than 50,000 vehicles, amidst clunky styling and quality issues.

Elon Musk Alienating Everyone

Making matters worse is the fact that Elon's personal brand has become toxic to progressive consumers across the world, as he has pivoted to the right. This has directly led to lost sales for Tesla, as many progressives (the target demographic for Tesla's environmental EVs) have vowed never to buy Tesla.

However, with Elon Musk now going after the Republican champion, Donald Trump, Elon Musk risks alienating Republicans and right-winged consumers. Will Elon end up alienating both progressives and conservatives? Should such a polarizing figure remain the CEO of Tesla?

Robotaxi Launch In 1 Week

Of course, supporters of Tesla like Ark Invest's Cathie Wood have moved past stagnant vehicle sales in 2025 and have pinned their valuation models on Musk's grandiose vision of robotaxis and humanoid robots.

In fact, ARK attributes more than 60% of its projected $7 trillion in 2029 market capitalization estimate for Tesla to the robotaxi opportunity, never mind that Tesla's full self-driving ("FSD") software has yet to achieve the L4 autonomy that is required for robotaxi services. (Figure 8).

Therefore, Tesla's June 12th target launch date for its robotaxi service will be a key 'make or break' catalyst for the company. Expectations are high, and Musk's outburst against Donald Trump could be a sign that he is buckling under the stress of meeting the deadline.

Conclusion

TSLT's performance in the past year succinctly demonstrates the risks and allure of leveraged ETF investments. TSLT's shares soared in 2024, as rising optimism over Tesla's prospects coupled with positive convexity allowed the ETF to return over 300% at one point. However, negative leverage as Tesla's sales slowed and volatility decay has also caused the TSLT ETF to lose over 60% YTD.

Overall, I believe the only winners in the leveraged ETF game are the fund managers who can earn big management fees and the market makers who earn trading commissions.

Investors who share Elon Musk's vision for robotaxis and humanoid robots are encouraged to buy actual shares and avoid the negative volatility decay. However, I fear Elon Musk may be alienating consumers both left and right, and there may be nobody left to buy Tesla products.

I continue to rate TSLT a sell.

data.title: Badger Meter: Not Worth 50x Earnings As Growth Starts To Slow
data.description:
I follow the water industry and have covered quite a few names in that space on Seeking Alpha over the years. I like to invest in both water utilities and also the companies that provide infrastructure for the water sector more broadly.

Water is attractive in that it's hard to disrupt technologically. It should also enjoy increased investment over time. For one, there is the need to replace aging water systems. With the Flint water crisis, for example, we saw that systemic underinvestment in this vital infrastructure can lead to significant human consequences. There are also likely to be higher regulatory thresholds for water service going forward, particularly in areas such as the treatment of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), the so-called forever chemicals. Environmental concerns are also likely to lead to more investment in water reuse and conservation efforts.

I have made the case for owning water infrastructure and services companies such as Ecolab (ECL) in the past, even at relatively high valuation multiples. I am willing to look past the starting price to some degree for a high-quality water company. That said, there's a limit to how far that generosity runs.

Today's example, Badger Meter (NYSE:BMI) falls into the category where the valuation has become difficult to justify. With the most recent run-up in the share price, Badger Meter is now trading at close to 55 times trailing earnings and about 50 times forward earnings. The Badger Meter has been on my watch list for a long time, and it's something I would like to own at the right entry point.

That said, we aren't anywhere near a suitable entry point today. In fact, we're getting to the level where even long-time shareholders might consider selling their positions because it's hard to imagine this company maintaining this valuation multiple going forward.

It's not just earnings, either. Consider that this water company is now up to nearly 9x revenues. Just look at this chart of Badger Meter's price-to-sales ratio over the past 25 years:

A significant chunk of Badger Meter's historic returns have come from multiple expansions in addition to the growth in its underlying earnings per share.

The Bull Case: Tailwinds And Improved Business Quality

For those unfamiliar, Badger Meter offers tools for measuring and keeping track of water usage. In addition to smart meters and related equipment for water utilities, Badger Meter has several other ancillary services. It has an increasing array of equipment and software systems for leak detection, water quality monitoring, and consumer engagement tools for monitoring and managing water usage.

The company had often grown revenues in the mid-single digits range historically, which is not too surprising given that it primarily sold water equipment, normally a fairly stable market.

However, growth greatly accelerated starting in 2021:

Specifically, while Badger Meter has averaged a 9% compounded growth rate over the past decade, this has surged to a 15% average growth rate over the past five years.

This has been driven in significant part due to its software offerings, which allow for more recurring revenue growth rather than the top line being driven by one-time hardware sales.

In addition, U.S. water utilities are in a heavy CAPEX cycle right now, in large part due to aging infrastructure. Some recent data suggests that about 19% of installed water mains are beyond their useful lives, with the average age of a failing water main being 53 years old. There are an estimated 260,000 pipe failures per year. After decades of underinvestment, there could be a large addressable market in trying to close this gap.

This tailwind has helped funnel more money toward Badger Meter, especially as it relates to water usage analytics and optimization expenditures. I believe some of that faster growth is likely to be sustainable. There's plenty of room left in the market for conversion to smart meters and more modernized water infrastructure. Future stakeholder demands around conservation and efficient water usage will only intensify. Put simply, industry tailwinds are strong, and Badger Meter isn't likely to return to low-to-mid single-digits revenue growth in the near future.

That said, I don't think the recent 15% annualized revenue growth rate is sustainable, either. Higher interest rates are likely to reduce capital expenditures on water systems, at least at the margins. I don't expect a huge tailwind from federal government spending on water projects under the current administration. And a softer economy, in general, could curtail willingness to spend at quite the same rate that we've just witnessed.

We are already seeing indications of this in the company's outlook. The company's Q1 results were strong, with revenues growing at 13.2% year-over-year and earnings per share topping expectations.

However, the company offered this more measured comment about its forward outlook:

"As previously communicated, we will face difficult prior-year comparisons in the upcoming second quarter of 2025. Nevertheless, the attractive fundamentals of the water industry, and our ability to add value to customers with an innovative and reliable portfolio of solutions, support our average high-single-digit long-term revenue growth outlook."

Straight from the horse's mouth, we see that Q2 is not likely to be particularly strong, at least not compared to Badger Meter's recent standard.

Additionally, management stated that its long-term revenue growth outlook is in the high single digits.

So, we know that Q2 is likely to be quite soft and a significant deceleration from the past year. In addition, investors should be planning on something closer to 8-9% long-term revenue growth, rather than the 15% annualized rate that Badger Meter has posted over the past five years. Despite the clear indication of a slowdown ahead, Badger Meter stock shot up to new all-time highs following that Q1 earnings report:

BMI Stock Verdict

Badger Meter is trading at or near record high valuations on metrics such as P/S, P/E, and EV/EBITDA.

It appears that investors are paying this lofty price due to Badger Meter's exemplary operating results over the past few years. That makes sense, to an extent.

However, the company has already noted that Q2 results are likely to be significantly softer and that the longer-term revenue growth rate will dip back into the high single digits.

I understand that Badger Meter has become a significantly better business over time. Recurring revenues tied to smart meters and related software products deserve a higher multiple than one-time revenues tied to individual meter sales. But there is some limit to how high the multiple can go. And I think we're near that point now that Badger Meter's revenue growth is about to decelerate.

I could see a case for paying 30-35x earnings for Badger Meter. The company is in the right place at the right time. There's a lot of structural demand for improved water infrastructure. And investors are willing to pay high multiples for predictable and consistent growth. Management has also done an excellent job operationally in terms of seizing the market opportunity and leaning into the demand for software solutions.

But let's be realistic here. BMI stock has already rallied more than 40% from the April lows. Shares are trading at 50x forward earnings and 8.5x revenues. This is a hard valuation to defend for a company whose own management has suggested that revenues will grow in the high single digits over the longer term.

data.title: XLU: A Look At The Structure And Composition Of This Popular Utility ETF
data.description:
The Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) is one of the largest and most popular exchange-traded funds tracking the domestic utilities sector. We can see this by looking at its assets under management and comparing the figure to some of its peers. Here is a chart that does so:

As we can see, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF is by far the largest index exchange-traded fund that focuses on the domestic utilities sector. Indeed, the second-largest exchange-traded index fund that tracks this sector (the Vanguard Utilities Index Fund ETF Shares) is less than half its size.

While most exchange-traded funds are highly liquid due to their open-ended structure, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF is especially so. It has an average daily volume of 11.87 million shares over the past three months. At a share price of $80.86, that means that approximately $959.808 million worth of shares change hands every day. As we can see from the chart above, this is a significant proportion of the fund’s total assets under management. As such, investors in the fund should not have any difficulty getting into or out of it at a moment’s notice. Indeed, an investor should typically be able to get into or out of this fund without having their trade have any real impact on the fund’s share price. Traders or other investors who value liquidity might value this aspect of the fund’s profile.

With that said, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF tracks an index of utility companies. Utilities are generally favored by more conservative investors who wish to have a degree of safety with their investments. Utility companies in general are very slow-growing entities that are unlikely to deliver the capital gains that we frequently see in other sectors of the economy. We can see this quite clearly by looking at the share price of XLU compared to the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the past ten years:

As is clearly shown, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF underperformed the S&P 500 Index on a price performance basis over the ten-year period that is shown. This is not atypical, as many, if not most, utilities do not grow their earnings at as high a rate as companies in some other sectors. It is rare to see a utility deliver more than 7% to 8% earnings per share growth annually, and some of them are lower than that. Thus, we would ordinarily not expect them to deliver the same level of capital gains as a technology company that is growing its earnings per share at a 10% or greater rate.

In order to provide investors with a competitive total return, utility companies tend to pay a significant proportion of their profits out to their investors in the form of dividends. The dividends that they pay represent an investment return that is in excess of any capital appreciation that their stock price delivers. When we combine this with the fact that many utilities have low valuations relative to other sectors (due to the slower earnings per share growth), it results in utility companies having fairly high yields compared to the rest of the market. We can actually see this by looking at the yields of the largest holdings of XLU. Here are the largest positions in the fund:

These are many of the largest electric and natural gas utility companies in the United States, which is pretty much what investors in this fund should expect. Here are the yields of these companies:

The Wall Street Journal provides the following yields for the major domestic broad-market indices:
